name: Frank — Bidirectional Effect Types Without Effect Variables
summary: Lindley, McBride, Hillerström, Convent (POPL 2014/2017; JFP 2020): strict functional language whose effect system achieves effect polymorphism without effect variables in source code. Bidirectional propagation threads the ambient effect set through type checking; multi-handlers interpret commands from several effect sources at once. Substrate for Unison's abilities.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Lindley, McBride, Hillerström, Convent et al.'s **Frank** (initial paper "Do Be Do Be Do", POPL 2014/2017; expanded JFP article "Doo Bee Doo Bee Doo", 2020) is a strict functional language whose distinguishing contribution is **effect polymorphism without effect variables in source code**. Where Koka and Effekt require programmers to write effect rows like `<exn,async>` explicitly on function types, Frank achieves the same expressive power through a *bidirectional* effect-typing rule: the effect set a function may perform is propagated structurally through type checking, and the source language has no syntax for naming an effect variable.

sources.label: Frank — Doo Bee Doo Bee Doo (JFP 2020)
Frank — Do Be Do Be Do (POPL 2017 arXiv)

sections.title: Adjustments and the bidirectional effect rule
sections.content:
The mechanical core is the **adjustment**. A Frank arrow `[E1] A -> [E2] B` says "in an ambient effect set adjusted by `E1` going in, returning a value of `B` adjusted by `E2`." Effect handlers (called *operators* in Frank) take continuation-style arguments and are pattern-matched against effect signatures; the typing rule for operator application threads the ambient effect set through the bidirectional check, and the language never needs an explicit `forall e. e in ...` quantifier the way ML-style row variables would.

This is the type-system reason Unison's *abilities* chose Frank as their basis: the bidirectional discipline scales better in practice than an explicit-row-variable ML extension would.

sections.title: Multi-handlers
sections.content:
**Multi-handlers** are Frank's other distinctive primitive: an operator can simultaneously interpret commands from *several* effect sources at once, without disturbing direct-style code. Where a Koka handler decides what to do with one effect at a time, a Frank multi-handler can specify the joint behaviour of (say) `state` and `nondet` together — useful for coordination patterns where the two effects must agree on action ordering.

Status (as of 2026-04): research-grade; Frank itself is a small implementation maintained by the authors at Edinburgh and Cambridge. The lesson generalises: **effect-row syntax in source code is not the only way to express effect polymorphism** — bidirectional propagation extended to effects is a viable alternative, and the same mechanism that lets a language elide many type annotations on values can elide them on effects. For language designers building from scratch, Frank is the existence proof that effect polymorphism does not require user-visible effect variables; the cost is that error messages must be carefully designed to expose the inferred effect set when bidirectional propagation fails.

sources.label: Frank — Do Be Do Be Do draft (March 2014)
Frank — Doo Bee Doo Bee Doo (JFP 2020)
